questions.question: Why is a slow origin often more dangerous than a fully down origin?
options: Because retries and failover may keep sending more traffic into it while masking the incident until overload spreads.
Because fully down origins never affect user latency.
Because slow origins automatically disable edge caching.
Because health checks cannot observe latency problems at all.
questions.explanation: Partial slowness often triggers the most damaging forms of amplification.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of origin shielding?
options: To smooth and localize traffic before it reaches origins, reducing blast radius and repeated direct hits.
To eliminate the need for health checks and failover logic.
To guarantee lower latency than local POP delivery in all cases.
To store all customer data permanently at the edge.
questions.explanation: Shielding is about protecting origin behavior and making edge traffic easier to control.

questions.question: Why is gradual health scoring often safer than a pure binary healthy/unhealthy decision?
options: It reduces false failovers and allows traffic shifts to reflect degraded states before full failure.
It makes retries unlimited for healthy pools.
It removes the need to measure tail latency.
It guarantees all POPs see identical health instantly.
questions.explanation: Many real incidents involve degraded behavior, not a clean up/down transition.

questions.question: Which signal best detects that the failover policy itself is becoming dangerous?
options: Retry budget burn and rising load on surviving origin pools.
Only total DNS query count for the company.
The number of support documents published.
Certificate issuance rate.
questions.explanation: A policy is risky when the mitigation path starts amplifying traffic or shifting overload.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of strict failback cooldowns?
options: They reduce routing flaps, but they may delay recovery to the preferred lower-latency origin path.
They guarantee zero stale health data everywhere.
They remove the need for local health snapshots.
They make shielding unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Cooldowns improve stability but can keep traffic on a non-preferred path longer.

questions.question: If POPs disagree sharply on whether an origin is healthy, what is the strongest design response?
options: Combine local observations with shared regional signals and alert on divergent failover behavior.
Force every POP to ignore local observations entirely.
Retry more aggressively from all POPs until opinions converge.
Disable observability to reduce decision noise.
questions.explanation: Health needs both local sensitivity and enough shared context to avoid chaotic routing.

questions.question: Why should origin selection decisions be explainable to operators and customers?
options: Because failover and shielding are product-visible behaviors, and invisible routing decisions are hard to debug or defend.
Because explainability replaces the need for metrics.
Because origins are always managed by the same platform team.
Because it guarantees customers will not misconfigure pools.
questions.explanation: A platform needs evidence for why traffic moved and why a request was handled a certain way.

questions.question: What is the hidden risk of cross-region failover when the destination region remains technically healthy?
options: It may overload the surviving region or create unacceptable cost and latency even while availability stays nominal.
It automatically fixes all retry storms.
It eliminates the need for admission control.
It ensures better tail latency for every tenant.
questions.explanation: Availability alone is not enough if the failover path creates new bottlenecks or cost spikes.
